rulings.Case Title: RICHARD HEVENER JR ET AL VS. SOCO WEST, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, January 7, 2025, in Department 304, Line 1. 2. Intervenor Travelers, as Insurer of Suspended Corporation Jack Baskin, Inc's Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant. 826, 855). Plaintiffs' evidentiary objections to Intervenor's Exhibit H in the Yuen Declaration (relevant portions of Deposition Transcript of Roland Hevener) are sustained. Intervenor failed to satisfy either the unavailability or interest-and-motive requirement of Evidence Code section 1292, subdivision (a). (See Gatton v. A.P. Green Services, Inc. (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 688, 692 [that deponent in prior asbestos exposure action involving different parties was not unavailable was sufficient ground alone to exclude his deposition in later action against a different defendant], disapproved on different grounds, Sweetwater Union High School Dist. v. Gilbane Building Co. (2019) 6 Cal.5th 931, 944; see also Jong v. Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc. (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 391, 396 ["deposition testimony taken in another case cannot be used to establish the truth of the deponents' testimony"].) Intervenor fails to sustain its initial burden of showing that Plaintiffs do not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence that Decedent was exposed to asbestos from any work performed by the employees of Intervenor's insured Jack Baskin, Inc. ("Baskin"). (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) Plaintiffs identified themselves, James Hevener III, Decedent, and James Hevener Sr. (deceased) as persons with knowledge regarding Baskin's actions at Decedent's jobsites. (Yuen Decl., Ex. D [Plaintiffs Responses to Defendant's Special Interrogatories, Set one] at 53:10-54:22.) Plaintiffs also identified Decedent's brother Ronald Hevener, as a coworker who worked besides Decedent. (Id. at 7:7-8.) The Court is not persuaded by Defendant's argument that the deposition of Mr. Hevener from a prior litigation sufficiently addressed the issues in this case. While Defendant asserts that Mr. Hevener was deposed over several days about the very sites in this case, the deposition did not specifically address the Decedent or the allegations at issue in this case. Moreover, Defendant had the opportunity to depose these witnesses in this action but chose not to do so. Intervenor offers no evidence that it deposed or attempted to depose Mr. Ronald Hevener (Decedent's brother) regarding what information he possess about Baskin and Decedent. In the absence of evidence that Mr. Ronald Hevener will be unable to testify that he recalls working in Decedent's presence with asbestos-containing products sold or supplied by Baskin, the court cannot find that Plaintiffs will be unable to establish asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant. (See Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Constr. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 81 [stating that "[c]ourts can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered"]; see also Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439 [a plaintiff's ignorance is by itself insufficient to demonstrate that plaintiff lacks the ability to produce evidence supporting her claim and does not serve to shift defendant's initial burden].) Even if Intervenor had carried its initial burden, Plaintiffs' written discovery responses and the Hevener Declaration give rise to triable issues of fact as to whether, as Plaintiffs allege, the decedent was exposed to asbestos-containing dust by Defendant's laborers sweeping up asbestos-containing joint compound in his presence. (See, e.g., Def. Ex. D, 3, 7; R. Hevener Decl. 8-9, 11.) Intervenor's evidentiary objections to the Hevener Declaration are overruled. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2.)

rulings.Case Title: RICHARD HEVENER JR ET AL VS. SOCO WEST, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, January 7, 2025, in Department 304, Line 1. 1. Defendant Anthony R. Morales (DBA A Morales Co.) Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant. Plaintiffs' evidentiary objections to Defendant's Exhibits H, I, J, K and L in the Yuen Declaration (relevant portions of Deposition Transcripts of Roland Hevener and John Allert) are sustained. Defendant failed to satisfy the unavailability requirement of Evidence Code section 1292, subdivision (a). (See Gatton v. A.P. Green Services, Inc. (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 688, 692 [that deponent in prior asbestos exposure action involving different parties was not unavailable was sufficient ground alone to exclude his deposition in later action against a different defendant], disapproved on different grounds, Sweetwater Union High School Dist. v. Gilbane Building Co. (2019) 6 Cal.5th 931, 944; see also Jong v. Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc. (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 391, 396 ["deposition testimony taken in another case cannot be used to establish the truth of the deponents' testimony"].) Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing that Plaintiffs do not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence that Decedent was exposed to asbestos from any work performed by Defendant's employees. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) Plaintiffs identified Roland Hevener and John Allert as persons with knowledge regarding Defendant's actions at Decedent's jobsites. (Yuen Decl., Ex. D [Plaintiffs Responses to Defendant's Special Interrogatories, Set one] at 91:15-92:26; 92:3-93:14.) Defendant offers no evidence that it deposed or attempted to depose Mr. Hevener and Mr. Allert. The Court is not persuaded by Defendant's argument that the depositions of Mr. Hevener and Mr. Allert from prior litigation sufficiently addressed the issues in this case. First, as noted, those deposition transcripts are inadmissible. Second, while Defendant asserts that both witnesses were previously deposed regarding job sites in which Plaintiffs claim Defendant is liable, Defendant was not a party to those prior actions, and the depositions did not specifically address the Decedent or the allegations at issue in this case. Moreover, Defendant had the opportunity to depose these witnesses in this action but chose not to do so. In the absence of evidence that Mr. Hevener and Mr. Allert will be unable to testify that they recall working in Decedent's presence with asbestos-containing products sold or supplied by Defendant, the court cannot find that Plaintiffs will be unable to establish asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant. (See Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Constr. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 81 [stating that "[c]ourts can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered"]; see also Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439 [a plaintiff's ignorance is by itself insufficient to demonstrate that plaintiff lacks the ability to produce evidence supporting her claim and does not serve to shift defendant's initial burden].) Even if Defendant had carried its initial burden, Plaintiffs' written discovery responses, the Hevener Declaration, and Defendant's own evidence give rise to triable issues of fact as to whether, as Plaintiffs allege, the decedent was exposed to asbestos-containing dust by Defendant's laborers sweeping up asbestos-containing joint compound in his presence. (See, e.g., Def. Ex. D, 2, 7; R. Hevener Decl. 8-10; Def. Ex. H, 119:4-9.) Defendant's evidentiary objections to the Hevener Declaration are overruled. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2).

rulings.Case Title: JANICE M. COOK ET AL VS. ANNING-JOHNSON COMPANY
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION And Notice Of Motion For Preference Pursuant To C.C.P. 36(A) And 36(D)
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, January 7, 2025, in Department 304, Line 2. The Court exercises its discretion and GRANTS Plaintiffs' Motion for Order Granting Preference in Setting Case for Trial, and Extending Discovery Cutoff under C.C.P. Section 36 (a) and (d). Plaintiffs provided clear and convincing medical documentation from Dr. Kjelland that Ms. Cook suffers from incurable malignant pleural mesothelioma, raising substantial medical doubt of her survival beyond six months. The Court finds that Ms. Cook's health is such that the interests of justice will be served by granting this preference. Opposition filed. Conditional Oppositions filed. Joinder filed. Reply filed. 1. The trial date is April 28, 2025, at 11:15 a.m. in Department 206. a. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. section 36(f) is May 7, 2025. b. The parties shall follow the California Rules of Court, San Francisco Local Rules, and Local Rule 20. 2. The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is April 22, 2025. a. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise. b. Before a party files and serves a summary judgment/adjudication motion, it must contact the clerk to make a reservation. c. The Court allows a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared, unless good cause is found to exceed this number. Contact the clerk to schedule a good cause hearing. 3. Time to respond to written discovery not yet served is shortened to 20 days. a. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier. b. Any issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, shall occur in person or via telephone, not by email or letter. 4. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service. 5. The fact discovery cut-off date is April 11, 2025. 6. The expert discovery cut-off date is April 25, 2025. 7. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than February 6, 2025. a. If Plaintiff submits documents to bankruptcy trusts after this date, they must notify Defendants no later than five days after submission. b. The fact discovery cut-off does not apply to bankruptcy documents. 8. No later than January 21, 2024: a. Plaintiff must provide a statement to each Defendant stating the name and contact information of each lay person with knowledge of any facts supporting Plaintiff's claims as to each Defendant. b. Defendants must provide a statement stating the name and contact information of each lay person with knowledge of facts supporting Defendant's affirmative defenses. c. Any person not identified on the statements may be barred from testifying at trial or presenting a declaration on a summary judgment motion. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2.)

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS JOSEPH CURRY VS. 3M COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Notice Of Motion & Motion To Quash Complaint For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, January 7, 2025, in Department 304, Line 3. DEFENDANT ROGERS CORPORATION'S MOTION TO QUASH COMPLAINT FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION. The Court's tentative ruling is to GRANT Defendant Rogers Corporation's motion to quash the complaint for lack of personal jurisdiction and to DENY Plaintiff's request that the Court continue the hearing to allow him to conduct jurisdictional discovery. California courts may exercise jurisdiction on any basis consistent with the Constitutions of California and the United States. (Code Civ. Proc. Section 410.10.) Jurisdiction is proper if a defendant has minimum contacts with California such that a suit here does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice. (Internat. Shoe Co. v. Washington (1945) 326 U.S. 310, 316.) "'Minimum contacts' may support either general (also called 'all-purpose') jurisdiction or specific (also called 'case-linked') jurisdiction." (SK Trading International Co. Ltd. v. Superior Court (2022) 77 Cal.App.5th 378, 386 ("SK Trading"); see Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. v. Superior Court (2017) 582 U.S. 255, 262 ("Bristol-Myers").) The paradigm of general jurisdiction for an individual is his or her domicile, and for a corporation its state of incorporation or principal place of business. (Daimler AG v. Bauman (2014) 571 U.S. 117, 137.) If general jurisdiction exists, the defendant may be sued in California whether or not its contacts with the state are related to the controversy alleged in the litigation. (See id.) On the other hand, a court "may exercise specific jurisdiction over a nonresident defendant only if: (1) the defendant has purposefully availed himself or herself of forum benefits; (2) the controversy is related to or arises out of the defendant's contacts with the forum; and (3) the assertion of personal jurisdiction would comport with fair play and substantial justice." (Pavlovich v. Superior Court (2002) 29 Cal.4th 262, 269 (cleaned up); Vons Companies, Inc. v. Seabest Foods, Inc. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 434, 445-447.) "The plaintiff asking the forum state to exert jurisdiction over the out-of-state defendant bears the initial burden of establishing the first two elements by a preponderance of the evidence, and if the plaintiff does so, the out-of-state defendant then bears the burden of convincing the court why the exertion of personal jurisdiction would not comport with fair play and substantial justice." (Jacqueline B. v. Rawls Law Group, PC (2021) 68 Cal.App.5th 243, 253; see Picot v. Weston (9th Cir. 2015) 780 F.3d 1206, 1211 [it is plaintiffs' burden to satisfy the first two factors. If plaintiffs meet their burden, then "the burden shifts to the defendant to set forth a compelling case that the exercise of jurisdiction would not be reasonable." (cleaned up)].) "To meet this burden, a plaintiff must do more than make allegations. A plaintiff must support its allegations with competent evidence of jurisdictional facts. Allegations in an unverified complaint are insufficient to satisfy this burden of proof." (Rivelli v. Hemm (2021) 67 Cal.App.5th 380, 393 (cleaned up).) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 5.)

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS JOSEPH CURRY VS. 3M COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT ROGERS CORPORATION MOTION / NOTICE OF MOTION & MOTION TO QUASH COMPLAINT FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY. PART 4 OF 4)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 4 of 5) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry.) DEFENDANT ROGERS CORPORATION MOTION / NOTICE OF MOTION & MOTION TO QUASH COMPLAINT FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY. PART 4 OF 5) Here, Plaintiff presents no evidence from which the Court could find that it has specific jurisdiction over Rogers. Plaintiff asserts that he is "informed and believes that from 1969 until 1980, Rogers supplied Square D with molding compounds reinforced with crocidolite, that Plaintiff believes would have been used in electrical equipment Mr. Curry would have worked with in California shipyards." (Gold Decl. 4 (emphases added).) However, speculation on "information and belief" in a declaration of counsel does not constitute admissible evidence sufficient to meet Plaintiff's burden. (See LG Chem, Ltd., 80 Cal.App.5th at 365 ["It is [plaintiff's] burden to prove this second prong of the specific jurisdictional analysis, and he must do that with competent evidence of jurisdictional facts" (cleaned up).) Plaintiff does not show that he was, in fact, exposed to asbestos through use of a product manufactured by Rogers. At most, Plaintiff's showing establishes that before 1985, Rogers manufactured a line of asbestos-containing products, phenolic molding compounds, used in electronic connectors and sold to a number of companies in that business including Square D. (Gold Decl., Ex. A.) Plaintiff makes no showing that Square D purchased (or used) those compounds in California, or that Plaintiff used or was exposed to specific products containing them, much less that Rogers itself had any direct contacts with California. "In order to prevail on a motion for a continuance for jurisdictional discovery, the plaintiff should demonstrate that discovery is likely to lead to the production of evidence of facts establishing jurisdiction." (In re Automobile Antitrust Cases I & II (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 100, 127 (emphasis added).) Plaintiff, who has not even shown that he was exposed to any particular asbestos-containing Rogers product in California, fails to indicate what specific discovery he intends to propound, much less that it is likely to support his position. He asserts only that he has not deposed Rogers' Person Most Qualified on "jurisdiction-related issues," and that he intends to propound unspecified "written jurisdictional discovery" on Defendant that he believes will "elicit information regarding Roger's contacts with California." (Opposition, 6.) That vague request is insufficient. (See Preciado, 867 Cal.App.5th at 972-973 [affirming denial of continuance to conduct jurisdictional discovery where plaintiffs "failed to identify any specific area of inquiry that they would pursue if they were allowed to conduct discovery"]; see also LNS Enterprises LLC v. Continental Motors, Inc. (9th Cir. 2022) 22 F.4th 852, 864-865 ["a mere hunch that discovery might yield jurisdictionally relevant facts" is an insufficient reason for a court to grant jurisdictional discovery (cleaned up)].) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 5 of 5.)

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS JOSEPH CURRY VS. 3M COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT ROGERS CORPORATION MOTION / NOTICE OF MOTION & MOTION TO QUASH COMPLAINT FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY. PART 3 OF 4)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 3 of 5) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry.) DEFENDANT ROGERS CORPORATION MOTION / NOTICE OF MOTION & MOTION TO QUASH COMPLAINT FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY. PART 3 OF 5) Second, "In order for a state court to exercise specific jurisdiction, the suit must arise out of or relate to the defendant's contacts with the forum." (Bristol-Myers, 582 U.S. at 262 (cleaned up).) "In other words, there must be an affiliation between the forum and the underlying controversy, principally, an activity or occurrence that takes place in the forum State and is therefore subject to the State's regulation." (Id. (cleaned up).) "For this reason, specific jurisdiction is confined to adjudication of issues derived from, or connected with, the very controversy that establishes jurisdiction." (Id. (cleaned up).) "When there is no such connection, specific jurisdiction is lacking regardless of the extent of a defendant's unconnected activities in the State." (Id. at 264.) However, a showing that a nonresident defendant sold its product into the California market and that plaintiff was injured by a product sold in California is not sufficient to show the requisite connection ("relatedness") between the plaintiff's products liability claims and the nonresident defendant's forum activities. (LG Chem, Ltd., 80 Cal.App.5th at 363-365.) Under Plaintiff's argument, "there would potentially be personal jurisdiction over a company in a consumer's product liability claim in any jurisdiction that is part of the product's distribution chain. That goes too far." (Id. at 368.) Rather, a plaintiff must offer evidence to show how or by whom the allegedly defective product was distributed to the customer, whose use caused plaintiff's injury. (Id. at 369-370.) The bare fact that defendant may have contracted to sell its product to California companies is not sufficient; "That is because, even regularly occurring sales of a product in a State do not justify the exercise of jurisdiction over a claim unrelated to those sales." (Id. at 370 (cleaned up); see also, e.g., Preciado, 87 Cal.App.5th at 984 [where allegedly defective bus chassis was assembled in South Carolina by defendant nonresident manufacturer, a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in South Carolina, and then sold in Michigan, and manufacturer had no involvement in bringing it, or the bus into which it was ultimately incorporated, into California, plaintiffs failed to meet their burden to establish that their claims against defendant arose out of ore relate to its contacts with California].) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 4 of 5.)

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS JOSEPH CURRY VS. 3M COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT ROGERS CORPORATION MOTION / NOTICE OF MOTION & MOTION TO QUASH COMPLAINT FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY. PART 2 OF 4)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 5) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry.) DEFENDANT ROGERS CORPORATION MOTION / NOTICE OF MOTION & MOTION TO QUASH COMPLAINT FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY. PART 2 OF 5) Defendant asserts, without offering any supporting evidence, that it is a Massachusetts corporation with its principal place of business in Chandler, Arizona, and has no places of business or employees in California. (Opening Brief, 2.) Plaintiff does not contend that Rogers is subject to general jurisdiction in California. (See Opposition, 3-5 [asserting only that the Court has specific jurisdiction over Rogers].) The Court finds that Plaintiff does not meet its burden of establishing that Rogers is subject to specific jurisdiction as to his claims. Plaintiff asserts that "[Rogers'] contacts are sufficient to support specific jurisdiction because it knew (or reasonably should have anticipated) that its asbestos product would be sold in California." (Opposition, 4.) The Court disagrees. Plaintiff relies on dated authority and fails to address more recent cases discussed in Defendant's opening brief, including Bristol-Myers and Ford Motor Company v. Montana Eighth Judicial District Court (2021) 592 U.S. 351. At a minimum, Plaintiff must show that the defendant "serves a market for a product in the forum State and the product malfunctions there." (Ford Motor Company, 592 U.S. at 363; see also id. at 371 [finding specific jurisdiction where "resident-plaintiffs allege that they suffered in-state injury because of defective products that [defendant] extensively promoted, sold, and serviced" in the forum states].) Plaintiff makes no such showing, nor does it meet its burden as to either of the first two prongs of the specific jurisdiction test. First, "California courts have consistently concluded that a foreign corporation purposefully avails itself of the benefits of the California forum when it knowingly sells and ships its products to California businesses for use in California." (LG Chem, Ltd. v. Superior Court (2022) 80 Cal.App.5th 348, 363.) Contrary to Plaintiff's argument, however, that Rogers may serve, directly or indirectly, a market for its product in California is insufficient to show purposeful availment. (Preciado v. Freightliner Custom Chassis Corp. (2023) 87 Cal.App.5th 964, 979.) The analysis requires a showing that the sale of the manufacturer's product is not simply an isolated occurrence, but arises from the manufacturer's efforts to serve the market in the forum state, that the defendant designed the product for the market in the forum state, established channels for providing regular advice to customers in the forum state, or marketed the product through a distributor who agreed to serve as its sale agent in the forum state. (Id. at 978-979 & fn. 10.) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 3 of 5.)
